content:
{
  "title": "Prompt Anchoring: Contextual Interleaving for Few-Shot Multilingual LLM Performance",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) underperform on low-resource or code-switched multilingual tasks, often failing to generalize from high-resource language prompts. Existing prompting methods struggle to transfer task instruction and context across linguistic boundaries.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM robustness on low-resource and multilingual tasks is critical for broadening access and equity in NLP technology. Current methods rely heavily on English-centric or monolingual exemplars, missing opportunities for cross-lingual transfer. New prompting strategies are needed to bridge gaps in instruction following and domain adaptation.",
  "method": "We propose Prompt Anchoring, a method that interleaves task exemplars in high-resource languages with semantically aligned but label-free context snippets from the target low-resource language within the same prompt. The high-resource examples provide explicit task guidance, while the unlabeled in-context anchors from the target language prime the model on linguistic features and topic, jointly leveraging cross-lingual transfer. Anchored prompts are constructed by alternating high-resource labeled examples and short, thematically matched, unlabeled target-language passages, followed by the target-language test query. This method enables the model to connect task instructions to the target language even without explicit supervision.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on XTREME and FLORES datasets, focusing on low-resource languages. Baselines include standard few-shot prompting and retrieval-augmented prompts. Metrics are accuracy and BLEU/F1 on classification and generation tasks, as well as cross-lingual transfer efficiency. Ablation studies will isolate the effect of anchor placement and language mixing."
}
